show_name: Software Engineering Radio - The Podcast for Professional Software Developers
title: SE Radio 652: Christian Mesh on OpenTofu
summary_one_sentence: Tech lead Christian Mesh joins Robert Blumen to discuss the origins of OpenTofu as a Terraform fork, exploring open-source licensing, HCL compatibility, and the project's future roadmap.

show_name: Software Engineering Radio - The Podcast for Professional Software Developers
title: SE Radio 620: Parker Selbert and Shannon Selbert on Robust Job Processing in Elixir
summary_one_sentence: Host Gavin Henry speaks with Parker and Shannon Selbert about building the Oban background job framework, exploring how Elixir and PostgreSQL enable robust, distributed job processing.

show_name: The Changelog: Software Development, Open Source
title: Leading GitHub to a $7.5 billion acquisition (Interview)
summary_one_sentence: GitHub CTO Jason Warner discusses the story behind Microsoft's $7.5 billion acquisition, leadership, and his thoughts on remote work and GitHub's engineering future.

title: Interview with Laurence Bradford (@learncodewithme), Part Two
summary_one_sentence: The host and Laurence Bradford conclude their interview discussing the software developer job market, finding career direction, and publishing.
notes: Description is quite sparse and dominated by the sponsor read, but provides just enough signal for career and software engineering.

title: Episode 100!
summary_one_sentence: The host celebrates the 100th episode by sharing four actionable tips for continuous personal and professional improvement as a developer.
notes: Excluded "Developer Tea" from key entities per the guideline rule to exclude the podcast's own name.

title: The Middle of Everything Ever
summary_one_sentence: A listener's question about the most average-sized thing in the universe leads to a friendly mathematical exploration with mathematician Steven Strogatz.
notes: Vocabulary gap resolved: 'mathematics' added to taxonomy.

show_name: Brain Science with Ginger Campbell, MD: Neuroscience for Everyone
title: BS 160 Neuroscience of Consciousness
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Ginger Campbell begins a four-part series on consciousness neuroscience, comparing ideas from Dehaene, Carroll, and Damasio on how the brain generates subjective experience.

title: Self-Care: Can You Hack Your Mood?
summary_one_sentence: Science Vs investigates popular self-care hacks like ice baths and gratitude journaling, asking experts whether the science actually supports them.

show_name: Brain Science with Ginger Campbell, MD: Neuroscience for Everyone
title: BS 152 Twelfth Annual Review Episode
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Ginger Campbell reviews 2018's nine neuroscience books and guests, covering memory, creativity, language, emotion, and the work of Eve Marder.
notes: Annual review episode — no single focused topic; medium quality because it is a highlight reel not a deep dive.

show_name: Brain Science with Ginger Campbell, MD: Neuroscience for Everyone
title: BS 210 Introduction to Neurotransmitters
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Ginger Campbell revisits neurotransmitter basics—neuron function and how a single molecule like dopamine can have wildly varied effects through multiple receptors.

title: The secret power of onboarding, w/Canva's Melanie Perkins
summary_one_sentence: Canva CEO Melanie Perkins explains how obsessive attention to onboarding—for users, investors, and employees—powered Canva's growth to 40 million monthly active users.

title: After devastating aid cuts, Mercy Corps keeps fighting, with Tjada D’Oyen McKenna
summary_one_sentence: Mercy Corps CEO Tjada D'Oyen McKenna describes leading her organization through devastating US aid cuts that rescinded two-thirds of programs, forcing layoffs while maintaining operations in Gaza.

title: Rapid Response: Make mistakes to make progress, w/GM's Gerald Johnson
summary_one_sentence: GM's Gerald Johnson argues that embracing mistakes and discomfort is essential to achieving an all-electric vehicle future and becoming the world's most inclusive company.

title: Business world predictions for 2026
summary_one_sentence: Masters of Scale host Bob Safian shares 2026 business predictions—AI reality checks, energy prices, immigration, World Cup—framed as debate starters rather than definitive forecasts.
notes: Prediction episode; host + producer casual chat, not a traditional interview but closest format.

title: John Cogan on Entitlements and the High Cost of Good Intentions
summary_one_sentence: Economist John Cogan traces US entitlement programs from Revolutionary War pensions to Social Security, warning about the unsustainable cost of financing baby boomer benefits.

title: Origins of Water
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Lydia Hallis explains how water molecules rode asteroids to early Earth 4.5 billion years ago, and why proving primordial water's existence is scientifically game-changing.
notes: Vocabulary gap resolved: 'planetary_science' added to taxonomy.

title: Jason and the Argonauts
summary_one_sentence: Author Caroline Lawrence explores the myth of Jason and the Argonauts with The Ancients, examining its legendary monsters, hero's journey structure, and enduring appeal.
notes: Vocabulary gap resolved: 'mythology' added to taxonomy.

title: ENCORE: The Fight for Women's Suffrage | Passing the Torch | 3
summary_one_sentence: A new generation of early-20th-century suffragists wins Western state victories while simultaneously excluding Black activists like Ida B. Wells from their own movement.

title: Dinosaurs: The Last Days
summary_one_sentence: Author Riley Black describes what happened the day an asteroid ended the dinosaurs 66 million years ago—shockwaves, years of darkness, extinction—and why birds and crocodiles survived.
notes: Vocabulary gap resolved: 'paleontology' added to taxonomy.

title: Hanukkah & the Maccabees
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Jodi Magness traces Hanukkah's origins to the Maccabean Revolt against the Seleucid Empire over 2,000 years ago—a story of Jewish civil war and religious resistance.

title: 100: NSO
summary_one_sentence: The episode investigates NSO Group's Pegasus spyware—sold to governments worldwide—and Citizen Lab's research exposing how it surveils journalists, activists, and dissidents.
notes: Vocabulary gap resolved: 'surveillance_and_privacy' added; cybercrime secondary removed (NSO sells to govts, not a cybercrime episode).

title: 131: Welcome to Video
summary_one_sentence: Andy Greenberg recounts how investigators used bitcoin tracing to dismantle a child sexual abuse darkweb website, based on his book 'Tracers in the Dark.'

title: 136: Team Xecuter
summary_one_sentence: Team Xecuter built a modchip empire enabling game console piracy—this episode tells how key member Gary Bowser's legal downfall was more extreme than anyone could believe.

title: #390: Why Insults Sting and How to Handle Them
summary_one_sentence: Philosopher Bill Irvine applies Stoic thinking to explain why insults sting and how to strip them of their power, using social media amplification as the modern context.
notes: Topic spans ethics, philosophy_of_mind, and mental_health; used ethics as primary due to Stoic philosophy framing.

title: How to Use Cold & Heat Exposure to Improve Your Health | Dr. Susanna Søberg
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Susanna Søberg explains how deliberate cold and heat exposure improve metabolism, cardiovascular health, and neurotransmitter release, comparing cold immersion versus showers and sauna types.

title: Want to Be Happy? Give Yourself Reasons to Admire Yourself
summary_one_sentence: Ryan Bush presents a new framework for happiness and depression, arguing that self-esteem built from genuine reasons to admire yourself is the variable most advice ignores.

title: Authority Is More Important Than Social Skills
summary_one_sentence: Behavioral analyst Chase Hughes argues that authority and influence emerge from who you are rather than learned social skills, and presents five factors that build personal authority.
notes: Topic ambiguous between habits_and_mindset, leadership, and behavioral psychology; used habits_and_mindset as primary.

title: The Essential Habits for Becoming an Agile, Vital, and Durable Human Being
summary_one_sentence: Dr. Kelly Starrett distills lessons from training elite athletes into ten foundational mobility and durability habits that anyone can use to maintain lasting physical health.

title: Putting a Price on Pollution
summary_one_sentence: NYT climate reporter Somini Sengupta explains the EU's ambitious carbon pricing legislation to cut emissions, and whether political will exists to see it through.

title: Lies, and the Liars who Lie About the Lies (Ep 1321)
summary_one_sentence: Conservative commentator Dan Bongino attacks Kamala Harris's speech as dishonest, criticizes Hollywood celebrities, and runs through news items on fact-checkers and coronavirus politics.
notes: Vocabulary gap resolved: 'political_commentary' added; opinion/punditry distinct from policy 'politics'.

title: Are They Spying On You? (Ep 1774)
summary_one_sentence: Dan Bongino covers growing digital surveillance threats alongside news items on Taiwan, Clinton-Russia collusion allegations, YouTube censorship, and Wuhan lab research.

title: The New Abortion Fight Before the Supreme Court
summary_one_sentence: Two NYT reporters explain how Biden's challenge to Idaho's abortion ban under federal emergency medical law (EMTALA) could set precedent far beyond one state's restrictions.

title: About That Explosive Video (Ep 1204)
summary_one_sentence: Dan Bongino recaps his on-air confrontation with Geraldo Rivera, accuses Biden of plagiarizing Trump's coronavirus plan, and covers Spygate and Democratic Party controversies.
